source.title: The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity
source.summary: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure.
source.author: Islamic Economics
source.body_text: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated for centuries on this principle. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled it. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery. Topics: Waqf Endowments, Al-Azhar University, Ottoman Empire, Colonial Dismantling, Cash Waqf Revival Series: Historical Islamic Economics (Part I) • Episode 6 of 6

x.single_post: New podcast: “The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity.” How waqf endowments supported hospitals, universities and water systems — and how colonial dismantling changed that. Listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6 #Waqf…
thread: Episode 6: “The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity.” This podcast traces how irrevocable endowments funded public goods across centuries. Listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
At the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of productive land was waqf property, sustaining hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure for communities.
The episode discusses major institutions—such as Al-Azhar and Ottoman külliyes—and the systematic dismantling of waqf by colonial administrations.
Measured history and ideas for recovery are explored. Read and listen at the source: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6 #Waqf #Ottoman

linkedin.post: Podcast: “The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity” (Episode 6, Historical Islamic Economics series). The episode examines how waqf endowments—irrevocable charitable trusts—supported public goods: hospitals, universities such as Al-Azhar, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure. At the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of productive land was waqf property. The podcast also covers the dismantling of waqf institutions under colonial administrations and considers implications for institutional memory and possible recovery. Read and listen at the source: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6

short_video.title: The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity
short_video.hook: How did one institution fund hospitals, universities, and water systems for centuries?
short_video.voiceover: Waqf were irrevocable endowments that funded public goods across the Islamic world. At the Ottoman peak, about one‑third of productive land was waqf, supporting hospitals, universities like Al‑Azhar, soup kitchens and waterworks. Colonial policies later dismantled many waqf structures. Listen to the full episode and read more at islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
